fixture.content_parity: Every framework row renders the same medium-page model: repeated cards, route links, stats, and review form fields.

scope.safe_public_claim: This medium fixture compares deterministic static route payloads for the same content shape. It does not prove broad framework replacement.

frameworks.runtime_claim: static first route with DXPK proof artifact, no route runtime
frameworks.note: DX-WWW fixture renders the medium route as source-owned static evidence plus a non-blocking proof packet model.

frameworks.runtime_claim: no island hydration included in this fixture
frameworks.note: Generous Astro-style static floor for the same medium content. No astro build was run.

frameworks.runtime_claim: no Svelte client bundle included in this fixture
frameworks.note: Generous Svelte prerender floor for the same medium content. No Vite or SvelteKit build was run.

frameworks.runtime_claim: HTML carries htmx-style attributes; external htmx runtime bytes are not fetched
frameworks.note: HTMX-shaped static fixture for the same medium content. It does not fetch htmx.org runtime code.

frameworks.runtime_claim: no React, RSC, router, font, image, or prefetch runtime included
frameworks.note: Generous Next.js static-export floor for the same medium content. No next build was run.

honest_findings: This is a medium route fixture, not a real Astro/Svelte/HTMX/Next production build.
The competitor rows are intentionally generous static floors and exclude common framework runtime, router, devtool, image, font, and hydration overhead.
DX-WWW should use this evidence to find payload direction, not to claim it beats every framework in every scenario.
A future browser suite must add real framework builds, real route navigation, hydration or interaction tests, and production CDN transfer measurements.
conclusion: DX-WWW now has a reproducible medium-route evidence fixture beyond tiny public pages. The next proof should run real framework builds for the same page model.
